For any website or service, a database is an indispensable element. So what is a database? Let's talk about it.
Let me start with an everyday analogy — it'll make introducing databases later easier and less abstract.

From childhood, how do we acquire knowledge? We mostly absorb knowledge through reading books, looking at images, listening to people speak, and so on. To draw an analogy, I'd say books are like databases.
The database concept is really similar to books. You might wonder…
"What about the human brain? Doesn't the brain count as a database?"

I personally think it's more like a "buffer/cache" concept. Every reader of this post has had the experience: after reading a book, you might still remember the content an hour later, three days later, even a week later.

But if you don't actively apply the knowledge or techniques from the book, after a month or half a year, will you still remember the content? Most people would say "no". That's why I think the human brain is more like a buffer/cache.

Databases, on the other hand, are more about "permanent" storage — like books. As long as you don't throw them away and keep them well (assuming no natural disasters), the book is always there for you to read again.

So many services you use rely on this concept. Take Google's login service — to use it, you have to register, and your registration data gets stored in Google's database. When you open a webpage and log in next time, Google checks against this database to verify your username and password.

While you're reading this article, the article content is also stored in a database. You'll notice URL patterns like the dynamic IDs we covered earlier — that ID is the database ID, used to look up data in the database and display it on the webpage.
You're probably curious about "buffer" and "cache". Let me spend a moment explaining them.
If you're a frontend dev, you're probably familiar with buffer and cache. I won't drag this out — let me describe them simply so we can quickly move on to the database section.
: similar concept to bookmarks — lets us quickly access specific pages or content. For web pages, it speeds up load times.
: also a database-like concept, but data is time-limited. When you close the browser, buffer data gets cleared. Think of it as "short-term memory".
I'm not going to spend too much time on buffer and cache — I just mentioned them so I added a brief explanation.
There are many databases out there, like:
MariaDB (developed by the original MySQL devs)
If I tried listing them all I'd never finish — there are tons of database types out there. But we can divide databases into two main categories: relational databases and non-relational databases.
To avoid information overload, I won't go too deep — just briefly. If you're interested, look up more details yourself.
Relational databases are pretty self-explanatory — they have relations (no kidding).
In a database, there are concepts of database, table, column, and row. Let's skip column and row for now and just talk about database and table.
For relational databases, say we have a TodoList project. We'd create a database called TodoList, then create a table called todo within it for storing todo data.
This project might also need user registration, so we know which user each todo belongs to. So we'd add a
Then users might involve login permissions — regular users vs super admins — so we'd add a
So we'd end up with three tables:
. There are relations between these — our users use permissions from
. This process is called a relation.
Of course this is just a simple example. In reality there's also the concept of one-to-many, many-to-many, etc. — I'm just briefly touching on it here.
Beyond that, relational databases mainly use SQL (Structured Query Language) to operate. SQL is also a language, but I won't cover SQL operations in this series.
Relational databases are also called RDBMS (Relational Database Management System).
"Wait! I know! It means no relations!"
Uh… you could say that, but it's not quite right. Non-relational databases also have relations, just in a different way.
First, Non-Relational Database is also called NoSQL. Many people think this means "no SQL syntax", but it's not.

NoSQL was originally called Non-SQL (later reinterpreted as Not Only SQL), meaning NoSQL doesn't completely avoid SQL — it's just a general term for databases different from relational ones. What does that mean?
It means you can also use SQL in your project.
"Since we can use SQL, why not just use a relational database?"
Great question. Let me briefly describe NoSQL's characteristics.

NoSQL has a relatively flexible and simple data structure. With a relational database, you'd need to define table columns first before writing data. With NoSQL, you can write data directly without defining columns first — so it's more flexible.
NoSQL also has a semi-structured architecture. When your data is more complex and variable, NoSQL is easier to use. In some designs, NoSQL also has better query performance.
So relational and non-relational databases each have their own strengths and use cases. You can also mix both in a single project — that's also fine.

If you're a frontend dev, NoSQL is easier to learn — it's simpler overall and you don't need much SQL syntax. So if you want to learn databases, start with NoSQL. Plus, NoSQL's data format is similar to JSON, which you'll find very familiar.
There are many ways NoSQL can be presented. The more common one is Document-Oriented Database — Firebase's Realtime Database is a good example.

JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation. It's a data format and an exchange format. Compared to XML, JSON is simpler and easier to read. Note: even though JSON's name is JavaScript Object Notation, it's not part of JavaScript — you can use JSON in other languages.
Other types include Key-Value Database, Column-Oriented Database, Graph Database, etc. — but if I went through each one individually, I'd probably write 30 more posts (lol).
So going forward, I'll only introduce Document-Oriented Database — it's more common and easier to learn.
That's a wrap for this post. In the next one we'll start (?) learning NoSQL.
